title: Page Grid Layouts for Journals
subtitle: Ruling dot-grid, lined, and square-grid pages using a light-box guide sheet
excerpt: Three grid types for blank journal pages: a 5 mm dot grid, a 6 mm line ruling, and a 5 mm square grid. A reusable master template and a light box make each one quick to rule on any blank paper.

glossaryTerms.definition: A page pattern of small regularly spaced dots, typically at 5 mm intervals, that provide alignment reference without the visual weight of ruled lines.
A pre-ruled or pre-printed guide sheet placed beneath blank paper and illuminated from below with a light box, so its grid shows through without marking the blank page.
A square grid with 5 mm spacing between lines in both directions, a standard size that allows neat handwriting at most nib widths.

content.text: Why rule your own pages

Pre-printed notebooks fix the grid for you. When you rule your own pages you can vary the density by section: wider lines where you write prose, tighter squares where you sketch small diagrams, dots everywhere else. The light-box method means the guide sheet does the work; you just mark the dots or lines through the blank page.
Making the master template
A
master template
is a single sheet of 130 gsm cartridge paper ruled with a full
5 mm grid
in dark pencil or fineliner. You make it once and use it many times.
Mark the top and bottom edges of your template sheet at 5 mm intervals using your ruler and pencil.
Rule vertical lines from top to bottom through each pair of marks. Use the metal ruler and a sharp pencil, pressing firmly so the lines are clearly dark.
Mark the left and right edges at 5 mm intervals. Rule horizontal lines across the full width.
Go over all lines with a dark fineliner once the grid is complete. The stronger the contrast, the more clearly it shows through the blank page on the light box.
Store the template flat inside a hardback book or folder to keep it from creasing. A creased template produces wobbly visible guides.
Method 1: Dot grid at 5 mm
Place the master template on the light box. Lay your blank journal page on top, aligning the corners.
Turn on the light box. The grid should be clearly visible through the blank sheet. If it is faint, the template lines are too light: go over them again with a fineliner before continuing.
At every grid intersection where you want a dot, press the pencil point lightly onto the blank sheet. You do not need to draw lines; the dot itself is the guide.
Work row by row, top to bottom. Keep the pencil pressure consistent so all dots are the same size.
A well-executed
dot grid
gives alignment reference without directing the eye along any particular axis. It suits both writing and sketching on the same page.
Method 2: Lined ruling at 6 mm

A 6 mm line spacing suits most adult handwriting. You can rule this directly, without the light box, by marking both sides of the sheet at 6 mm intervals and ruling across with a ruler and light pencil. Alternatively, use a template with 6 mm horizontal lines drawn over the 5 mm grid sheet.
Mark 6 mm intervals down the left margin of the blank page.
Rest the ruler against each mark and rule a light line across to the right margin.
Leave the top 20 mm blank as a header zone.
Use an H pencil. HB leaves a mark that photographs too dark and can dirty the side of your hand as you write over it.
Method 3: Square grid at 5 mm
For a full square grid, use the master template and light box. This time, skip marking dots and rule directly along the template lines.
Place blank sheet on light box over template.
Trace all vertical lines using your ruler. Hold the ruler firmly. Rule in one smooth stroke from edge to edge.
Rotate the sheet 90 degrees. Trace all horizontal lines.
A ruled grid is heavier visually than a dot grid on the same page. Use it for sections that need tight alignment, such as tables, small maps, or a habit tracker.
Variations and combinations

A page does not have to use just one grid type. Rule the top half as lines for writing and add a 5 mm square grid at the bottom for sketching. Use the master template to set the boundaries, then rule only the sections you need.

items.symptom: Grid does not show through the blank paper on the light box
items.cause: Template lines are too faint or blank paper is too thick
items.fix: Redraw template lines with a dark fineliner. If the paper is very thick (200 gsm+), switch to the direct marking method instead of light-box tracing.

items.symptom: Ruled lines go slightly diagonal across the page
items.cause: Template and blank sheet shifted during ruling
items.fix: Fix both sheets to the light box with a small piece of washi tape before starting. Remove the tape after ruling.
Sharpen the pencil to a fine point before starting and re-sharpen after every 50 to 60 dots.
